title: The pseudogap in high-$T_c$ superconductors from SU(2) gauge symmetry and dynamic correlation effects
authors: I. A. Goremykin, A. A. Katanin
abstract: We consider the spectral properties of the two-dimensional Hubbard model, describing the electronic properties of high-$T_c$ compounds, within the SU(2) gauge theory, which assumes the separation of electronic degrees of freedom into those of spinon and chargon subsystems. We use the dynamic mean-field theory (DMFT) approach to describe magnetic long-range order in the chargon subsystem while also treating spinon fluctuations on top of this state. We show that DMFT supplemented by long-wavelength magnetic fluctuations is essential for describing the asymmetry in the damping between the inner and outer regions of the hole pockets and the resulting formation of Fermi arcs in the underdoped regime, especially at low hole doping. The underlying hole pockets in the chargon subsystem can be associated with those observed in quantum oscillation measurements.
summary: This theoretical work models high-Tc superconductors using an SU(2) gauge theory applied to the Hubbard model, separating electrons into chargon and spinon components. By combining DMFT with spinon fluctuations, the authors show that magnetic correlations are essential for explaining the characteristic pseudogap physics, specifically the formation of asymmetric Fermi arcs in the underdoped state.
main_problem: The paper addresses the pseudogap phenomenon in high-temperature superconducting cuprates, aiming to explain the spectral reconstruction and the formation of Fermi arcs.
main_result: The inclusion of long-wavelength magnetic fluctuations beyond standard DMFT is crucial, as it generates an asymmetry in the damping that successfully describes the formation of Fermi arcs in the underdoped regime.
method: The authors employ an SU(2) gauge theory framework applied to the Hubbard model, utilizing Dynamic Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) for the chargon subsystem supplemented by spinon fluctuations.
model_or_system: The system is the two-dimensional Hubbard model describing high-Tc superconductors, which is theoretically mapped onto a fractionalized description involving chargons and spinons within an SU(2) gauge theory.
key_observables: Spectral weight, damping asymmetry, Fermi arcs (observed in ARPES), and small, closed Fermi surface pockets (from quantum oscillation measurements).
important_parameters: Low hole doping regime, temperature ($T=0.1t, 0.2t$), and the coupling strength $U$ relative to the hopping $t$.
main_assumptions: The approach assumes the physical state can be mapped onto chargons with long-ranged magnetic order and small fluctuations of the gauge field $R_x$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate chargon spectral functions and pseudogap formation as doping changes, showing the transition from a hole pocket to an arc-like structure due to correlation effects.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical framework (SU(2) gauge theory/Hubbard model) to applying DMFT supplemented by spinon fluctuations, culminating in the demonstration that these correlations are necessary to explain the observed asymmetric spectral weight transfer leading to Fermi arcs.
why_interesting: The detailed treatment of gauge symmetry and the explicit inclusion of spinon dynamics provide a sophisticated theoretical mechanism for understanding spectral features (like Fermi arcs) that are hallmarks of strongly correlated quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
